items.q: Why are dried foods less prone to spoilage?
items.correct: Low water activity prevents microorganisms from growing.
items.misconception: Bacteria do not like dry textures.
items.why: Students think it's about texture preference. Microbes need available water. Drying reduces WATER ACTIVITY below the threshold microbes can use - no water = no metabolism = no spoilage.

items.correct: A protein containing all nine essential amino acids in adequate amounts.
items.misconception: A protein with the most calories.
items.random_wrong: Any protein from animal sources only.
items.why: Students think 'complete' means 'high quantity' or 'animal'. It means containing all ESSENTIAL amino acids the body can't make. Most animal proteins are complete; many plants need combining (e.g., rice + beans).

items.q: Why must the 14 major allergens be highlighted on UK food labels?
items.correct: Reactions can be severe or life-threatening, so consumers must identify them at a glance.
items.misconception: To advertise that the food contains them.
items.why: Students miss the consumer-safety logic. Labelling laws require allergens to be visibly listed (bold/highlighted) so allergic people can quickly check. Penalties for non-compliance can be severe.

items.q: The most common UK food-poisoning bacterium is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Campylobacter. “Salmonella” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Listeria can grow at temperatures as low as:
items.why: Correct answer: 0 °C. “5 °C” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If Campylobacter causes around 280,000 UK cases/year, daily cases (average, in thousands) ≈:
items.why: The real figure is 767. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
